medications.indication_rationale: A low-dose tricyclic is a recognised neuropathic-pain agent for CRPS, but starting it should follow in-person review; the source's plan to start it remotely in a person living alone with a high fall risk needs caution because of sedation and falls risk.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 10 mg orally at night (as stated in the source note)
contraindications_in_this_case: Sedation/falls risk in a person living alone with an unsafe home environment
Should not substitute for urgent in-person exclusion of DVT/compartment syndrome

medications.indication_rationale: A COX-2 inhibitor may be used for pain/inflammation, but the patient has already had no response to a non-selective NSAID (ibuprofen) at maximum dose; adding another NSAID class has limited expected benefit and its own risks.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 200 mg orally daily (as stated in the source note)
contraindications_in_this_case: Recent maximal ibuprofen use — cumulative NSAID exposure/GI and renal risk
NSAID monotherapy already ineffective for this neuropathic pain

medications.indication_rationale: The source proposes low-dose cannabinoids as targeted therapy, but evidence for CRPS is weak, they are not first-line, and initiating them remotely without specialist involvement is inappropriate.
contraindications_in_this_case: Not appropriate to initiate via telehealth without specialist pain review

medications.indication_rationale: Vitamin C has been studied for CRPS prevention after fracture/surgery, not as treatment of an established acute flare; presenting it as targeted acute therapy is not evidence-based.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Specialised pain/CRPS physiotherapy (graded motor imagery, desensitisation, mobilisation) is a cornerstone of CRPS management, arranged once acute mimics are excluded.
Home-safety and functional assessment for a person living alone with high fall risk and cluttered trip hazards.

integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Mirror therapy and graded motor imagery have supporting evidence in CRPS as part of a rehabilitation programme, but are adjuncts within specialist-guided physiotherapy, not a stand-alone acute solution and not a substitute for excluding dangerous mimics.

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: High-dose Vitamin C as acute flare treatment
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: No evidence supports high-dose Vitamin C for treating an established CRPS flare; it should not be presented as targeted therapy.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Watch for and immediately report any change to pallor, coldness, numbness or loss of movement in the foot, or new fever/spreading redness.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Enables early detection of limb-threatening ischaemia/compartment syndrome or systemic infection while awaiting in-person review.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Reduce fall risk tonight — clear the path to the bathroom, use an over-bed cradle to keep bedding off the limb, and avoid unassisted weight-bearing.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The patient lives alone with high fall risk and severe functional loss.

patient_education_points.point: Your symptoms most likely reflect a severe nerve-pain flare in the injured leg, where the nerves are over-firing so that even light touch registers as pain — but because the leg is swollen and painful after a recent fracture, a clot (DVT) and a pressure problem in the muscles (compartment syndrome) still need to be ruled out in person today.

Simple painkillers like paracetamol and ibuprofen often don't help this type of nerve pain; different (nerve-targeting) medicines and hands-on rehabilitation are usually needed, ideally started after you are examined.

Early treatment and rehabilitation are important to reduce longer-term nerve pain and skin/tissue changes; this usually takes weeks of multidisciplinary therapy and flares can settle with targeted treatment. (Delivered via chat and summarised by SMS.)
Keep bed sheets off the leg (e.g. an over-bed cradle), keep the leg comfortable, and avoid anything touching or pressing the foot until you are seen.

follow_up_plan.interval: Urgent same-day in-person review; then GP review within about 3 days to check medication response and coordinate specialist pain-clinic referral (per source).
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Completion of DVT/compartment-syndrome exclusion and imaging results, analgesic response, neurovascular status, home safety, and specialist referral.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any limb pallor/coldness/numbness/loss of movement, new fever or spreading redness, or uncontrolled escalating pain.

must_include_items: Recognise pain out of proportion with allodynia and vasomotor/sudomotor change in a post-fracture limb as most consistent with CRPS Type I
Actively screen for and arrange exclusion of DVT and acute compartment syndrome before accepting the CRPS label
Arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment because discriminating exam findings and imaging are not obtainable via telehealth
Give explicit safety-netting for limb-threatening (pale/cold/numb/pulseless) and infective (fever/spreading redness) deterioration with a call-000 threshold
Address the person's fall risk and living-alone context in the plan
acceptable_alternatives: Referring directly to ED rather than urgent care if same-day in-person review is otherwise unavailable
Deferring initiation of a neuropathic agent to the in-person clinician
errors_of_omission: Failing to consider or exclude DVT/compartment syndrome
Providing no deterioration safety-netting or no call-000 threshold
errors_of_commission: Confidently diagnosing CRPS remotely and discharging to routine follow-up only
Initiating sedating amitriptyline remotely without in-person review in a high-falls-risk patient
Recommending cannabinoids or high-dose Vitamin C as targeted acute therapy
Adding a second NSAID class as if it will resolve the crisis
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A passing response names CRPS as most likely WHILE arranging urgent in-person mimic exclusion and giving clear deterioration safety-netting. Automatic fail if the response excludes nothing dangerous and discharges to routine care, or if it initiates non-evidence-based therapy (cannabinoids/high-dose Vitamin C) as standard.
